headline: Spain claims second World Cup
deck: Ferran Torres scored in the 106th minute as Spain beat Argentina 1-0 after extra time on 19 July. The champions returned to Madrid the next day for a reception by the King and prime minister, then a parade estimated near two million strong. Individual awards and a tragic death in Ciudad Rodrigo framed the aftermath.
body: Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 after extra time in the World Cup final on 19 July, Ferran Torres striking the winner in the 106th minute. The goal secured the national team its second world title and the second star on the shirt. Luis de la Fuente’s players had already controlled long stretches of the match before the decisive moment arrived. The result closed a tournament that had steadily built toward this outcome. [E1]

Spain dictated the tempo throughout the final and limited Argentina to almost no clear openings. Possession remained heavily weighted toward the European side while the South Americans struggled to advance the ball with purpose. Unai Simon faced only scattered threats across the full 120 minutes. Torres’s finish arrived as the logical product of that sustained pressure rather than a sudden rupture. [E1]

The victory delivered Spain’s first world crown since 2010 and completed a double with the Euro 2024 title already in hand. De la Fuente’s squad had carried a long unbeaten run into the competition and converted that consistency into knockout-stage control. The second star now sits beside the European championship as the central achievement of this group of players. Records at the governing body confirm the scale of the feat. [E1][E2]

Rodri collected the Golden Ball as the tournament’s outstanding performer, while Unai Simon took the Golden Glove for his work in goal. Pau Cubarsi received the Young Player award, and Lamine Yamal continued to carry generational billing for the influence he exerted across the matches. Ferran Torres stood as the decisive figure once the final whistle sounded. Those individual honors matched the collective depth that had carried the side. [E2]

The squad returned to Madrid on 20 July and was received by King Felipe VI at the Palacio de la Zarzuela. Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez then welcomed the players at La Moncloa before an open-top bus carried them to the Plaza de Cibeles. Government estimates placed the crowd near two million as red and yellow filled the route. The Spanish federation documented the full sequence of the homecoming. [E3][E4][E5]

A 13-year-old boy died in Ciudad Rodrigo when a fountain collapsed during local celebrations, prompting three days of mourning in the town. Scattered arrests occurred in other cities amid the wider revelry. Joy and grief therefore travelled on parallel tracks across the country. Spanish reporting recorded both the confirmed death and the official period of mourning without inflating early injury counts. [E6]

The second star now crowns a generation that has claimed the two major international trophies in rapid succession. For a nation that waited sixteen years, the open-top bus under a sea of flags supplies the lasting image. The memory of the boy in Ciudad Rodrigo travels with those scenes as well. Spain’s triumph arrives complete with its human cost, held in the same national frame. [E3][E6]

evidence_box.fragment: Ferran Torres extra-time winner secures Spain’s second title
post-final awards and tournament records confirmed
squad’s return and the Madrid celebration documented
King Felipe VI receiving the team at Zarzuela
Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez receiving the squad
death of a 13-year-old in Ciudad Rodrigo
